claims.claim_text: Quantization can reduce memory footprint enough to make larger models workable on smaller hardware, but it comes with trade-offs.
claims.span_hint: lines 92, 374 | sections: VRAM: The First Hard Wall For Local Models; Did You Know
claims.rationale: The Transformers bitsandbytes guide explicitly says quantization reduces memory footprint, makes larger models workable on limited hardware, and documents trade-offs around supported operations and performance behavior.

claims.claim_text: Batch size affects GPU memory usage, and the feasible batch size depends on the GPU and model data type.
claims.span_hint: lines 92, 102 | section: VRAM: The First Hard Wall For Local Models
claims.rationale: The official Transformers GPU training guide directly states that batch size affects memory usage and that viable batch size depends on the GPU and data type.

claims.claim_text: Longer sequence or context lengths can spike memory usage and lead to out-of-memory errors.
claims.span_hint: lines 94, 100-102 | section: VRAM: The First Hard Wall For Local Models
claims.rationale: The TRL memory guide explicitly says large `max_length` values can spike memory usage and cause OOM errors.

claims.claim_text: When GPU memory is insufficient, quantized models can offload weights between GPU and CPU memory to fit larger models.
claims.span_hint: line 92 | section: VRAM: The First Hard Wall For Local Models
claims.rationale: The bitsandbytes guide documents CPU offloading for 8-bit models specifically as a way to fit very large models into memory.

claims.claim_text: Quantized LoRA-style methods can make some single-GPU fine-tuning workloads practical.
claims.span_hint: line 98 | section: VRAM: The First Hard Wall For Local Models
claims.rationale: The QLoRA paper directly states that its approach reduces memory usage enough to fine-tune a 65B model on a single 48GB GPU.

claims.claim_text: The module claims `nvidia-smi` exposes GPU memory, utilization, temperature, and power information during a run.
claims.span_hint: line 273 | section: Reading Symptoms Like An Engineer
claims.rationale: This tool behavior is real and documented in NVIDIA's official `nvidia-smi` documentation, but that host is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module claims many AI libraries are easiest to use on NVIDIA CUDA today.
claims.span_hint: line 360 | section: Hardware Specifics Without Turning This Into A Shopping List
claims.anchor_text: Many AI libraries are easiest on NVIDIA CUDA today, but other platforms can be reasonable depending on framework support, operating system, model format, and workload.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Library and hardware compatibility varies by framework, operating system, model format, and workload.
claims.rationale: This broad ecosystem-ranking claim is load-bearing but not backed here by a primary source that honestly carries the comparison.

claims.claim_text: The operating-system comparison asserts Linux is the common AI engineering default, macOS is strong for some local inference workflows, and Windows works with WSL-based workflows.
claims.span_hint: line 366 | section: Hardware Specifics Without Turning This Into A Shopping List
claims.anchor_text: Linux is common for AI engineering because many tools assume it, servers run it, and GPU documentation often targets it. macOS can be excellent for development and some local inference workflows. Windows can work, especially with WSL-based workflows, but you must be deliberate about paths, drivers, and environment boundaries.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Different AI toolchains have different platform support, so choose an operating system that matches the frameworks you plan to use and be deliberate about drivers, paths, and environment boundaries.
claims.rationale: This bundles several comparative platform claims that are too broad to leave uncited and too mixed to support cleanly with one authoritative allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: Fine-tuning workflows commonly produce saved checkpoints and training logs that consume storage.
claims.rationale: The Transformers Trainer docs explicitly describe checkpoint directories and built-in training logging behavior.

claims.claim_text: PEFT workflows save adapter artifacts as separate files rather than saving the full base model by default.
claims.rationale: The PEFT checkpoint guide directly documents that `save_pretrained()` stores adapter files and configuration separately from the base model.

further_reading.title: Transformers Bitsandbytes Quantization Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Best current reference for quantization, hardware compatibility, and CPU offloading when local GPU memory is limited.

further_reading.title: Transformers GPU Training Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for understanding how batch size and other single-GPU settings affect memory pressure on a home workstation.

further_reading.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary paper for the claim that quantized parameter-efficient fine-tuning materially changes what one GPU can handle.

notes: No shared citation pool exists for ai-ml-engineering/prerequisites, so all supported claims use newly proposed URLs. Skipped the fictional Maya and Jordan learner scenarios, quiz material, hands-on exercise steps, diagrams, code blocks, and general pedagogical advice.
